title: REST API uses weaker capability check than AJAX path — Authenticated (Author) users can reorder all posts of any type
description: The plugin exposes two paths for page reordering: an AJAX handler and a REST API endpoint. The AJAX handler (ajax_simple_page_ordering, line 517) correctly requires the edit_others_posts capability via check_edit_others_caps(), which restricts the operation to Editors and above. The REST endpoint's permission callback (rest_page_ordering_permissions_check, line 858) only checks current_user_can('edit_post', $post_id), which is satisfied by Authors/Contributors who own the specified post. The core page_ordering() function then calls wp_update_post() on all sibling posts without any per-post authorization check, modifying menu_order on posts the authenticated user does not own.
taint_trace: -> wp_update_post(sibling->ID, menu_order) [lines 663-714]: no cap check on sibling
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker has Author role on a WordPress site with simple-page-ordering installed. 2. Attacker owns any published/draft post. 3. Attacker sends: POST /wp-json/simple-page-ordering/v1/page_ordering with body: {id: <attacker_owned_post_id>, previd: false, nextid: <any_other_post_id>}. 4. Permission check passes (attacker can edit their own post). 5. page_ordering() queries all siblings of $post_id and calls wp_update_post() on each, updating menu_order to new values. Result: attacker scrambles the ordering of all pages/posts on the site, including administrator-owned content.
false_positive_analysis: Confirmed true positive. The capability discrepancy between AJAX (edit_others_posts) and REST (edit_post) paths is concrete and unambiguous. The rest_page_ordering_permissions_check function explicitly uses current_user_can('edit_post', $post_id) at line 858 while the AJAX equivalent at line 517 uses check_edit_others_caps(). WordPress Author role has edit_post capability on their own posts but not edit_others_posts. The REST API is publicly documented and easily discoverable.
